title: Pele and the Sacred Anger of Volcanoes
dek: Your fury is not a mistake, nor is it a moral failing. It is the earth moving beneath your skin, the deep tectonic shift of a body that refuses to be silent anymore. Like the volcano, your anger is a sacred force of creation and destruction, demanding to be felt in the jaw, the psoas, and the breath.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Tectonic Plates of Your Body


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.225Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake up at 3:14 AM, and the heat is already there. It does not start in your mind. It starts in the deep, dense musculature of your pelvis, radiating upward through the psoas, gripping the diaphragm, and finally locking your jaw. The masseter muscle - the strongest muscle in your body by weight - clenches with the force of a tectonic plate grinding against another. You have been taught to call this anxiety. You have been taught to call this stress. You have been taught to drink a glass of water, take a deep breath, and go back to sleep. But your body knows the truth. This is anger. This is the sacred, molten fury of Pele, the Hawaiian goddess of volcanoes, waking up inside your own fascia.

We are terrified of volcanoes because they destroy. We are terrified of our own anger for the exact same reason. But destruction is not inherently evil. When Pele erupts, she burns the old growth, yes. She consumes the dead wood, the stagnant structures, the things that have overstayed their welcome. But in her wake, she leaves the most fertile soil on earth. Your anger is no different. It is not a pathology. It is biology. It is the earth moving beneath your skin, demanding that you pay attention to what is no longer working, what is no longer safe, what is no longer true.

When you feel that heat rising, your first instinct - trained into you by decades of polite society, by teachers who told you to use your "inside voice," by a culture that prefers women to be small and accommodating - is to swallow it. You tighten your throat. You round your shoulders. You compress your chest. You try to cap the volcano. But the magma does not disappear just because you refuse to look at it. It simply finds another way out. It becomes a migraine. It becomes irritable bowel syndrome. It becomes a chronic ache in the levator scapulae, the muscle that connects your neck to your shoulder blade, carrying the weight of all the words you never said.

## The Wisdom of the Magma

Bessel van der Kolk, in his groundbreaking work on trauma, reminds us that the body keeps the score. He writes about how the physical body absorbs the impact of our unexpressed emotions, storing them in our tissues, our nervous system, our very posture. When you swallow your anger, you are not eliminating it; you are simply relocating it. You are moving it from the realm of expression into the realm of somatic burden. Van der Kolk's research shows that trauma and suppressed emotion physically alter the brain and the body, changing how we breathe, how we move, and how we connect with others. Your anger, when trapped, becomes a ghost that haunts your own house.

But what if you didn't trap it? What if you allowed the magma to rise?

I am not talking about screaming at the barista or throwing a plate against the wall. That is the explosion of a capped volcano, the desperate, uncontrolled release of pressure that has built up for too long. I am talking about the slow, deliberate, conscious acknowledgment of the heat. I am talking about feeling the anger in your body without immediately trying to fix it, spiritualize it, or manifest it away. There is no "love and light" here. There is only the raw, undeniable reality of your own nervous system.

When you allow yourself to feel the anger, you might notice the smell of your own sweat changing. It becomes sharper, more acrid. This is the smell of cortisol and adrenaline, the chemical messengers of your fury. You might notice your vision narrowing, your heart rate accelerating. This is your body preparing for action. This is your body telling you that a boundary has been crossed, that a truth has been ignored, that a change is required.

## The Herbalist's Cabinet for the Erupting Body

As an herbalist, I do not use plants to suppress the volcano. I use them to support the body as it processes the heat. When the anger is hot and stuck in the liver - the organ that Traditional Chinese Medicine associates with anger and frustration - I turn to bitter, cooling herbs. Dandelion root, burdock, and yellow dock. These are not delicate, flowery herbs. They are deep-rooted, tenacious plants that know how to dig into the dark soil and pull up what is needed. They stimulate the liver, encouraging it to process and release the metabolic waste of our fury.

If you are feeling the heat in your chest, if your heart is racing and your breath is shallow, you might reach for motherwort. *Leonurus cardiaca*. The lion-hearted mother. Motherwort does not sedate you. It does not make you forget why you are angry. Instead, it holds your heart steady while you feel the fire. It gives you the courage to stay present with the intensity of your own emotion. You can find support in [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20), which details how these somatic responses are deeply embedded in our physiology.

And when the anger has left you exhausted, when the eruption is over and you are left in the ash, that is when we turn to the nervines. Skullcap, milky oats, ashwagandha. These herbs rebuild the frayed edges of the nervous system. They nourish the soil that the fire has cleared. They remind the body that it is safe to rest now.

## The Somatic Practice of the Volcano

Here is a practice for when the heat is rising. Do not do this in your mind. Do this in your body.

Find a place where you can stand with your feet hip-width apart. Take off your shoes. Feel the floor beneath you. Notice the points of contact between your soles and the earth. 

Bring your attention to your jaw. Is it clenched? Do not force it open. Just notice the tension. Notice the masseter muscle. 

Now, bring your attention to your belly. Is it tight? Are you holding your breath? 

Imagine a line of heat running from the center of the earth, up through the soles of your feet, up through your legs, into your pelvis. This is the magma. 

Allow your knees to bend slightly. Allow your pelvis to drop. 

Take a breath in, not into your chest, but deep into your belly. As you exhale, make a sound. Not a polite sound. A low, guttural sound. A groan. A growl. Let the sound vibrate in your chest and your throat. 

Do this three times. 

Notice what shifts in your body. Notice if the heat has moved. Notice if the jaw has softened, even a fraction of a millimeter. You are not trying to get rid of the anger. You are simply giving it a channel to move through. You are allowing the volcano to breathe.

## The Ancestral Fire

Resmaa Menakem teaches us about the ancestral nature of our somatic responses. In his work on somatic abolitionism, he explains how trauma, fear, and anger are passed down through generations, not just in our stories, but in our very DNA, in the way our nervous systems are wired. The anger you feel in your jaw, in your psoas, may not just be your anger. It may be the anger of your mother, your grandmother, the women who came before you who were not allowed to speak, who were not allowed to erupt. 

When you feel your anger, you are feeling the accumulated heat of generations. You are the volcano that is finally allowed to vent. This is a profound responsibility, and a profound gift. By feeling the anger in your own body, by allowing it to move through you without destroying yourself or others, you are metabolizing the ancestral trauma. You are breaking the cycle of suppression. You are doing the work that your ancestors could not do.

Menakem emphasizes that this work cannot be done purely cognitively. You cannot think your way out of ancestral trauma. You must feel your way out. You must allow the body to process the charge. This requires slowing down. It requires tuning in to the subtle sensations of the body - the fluttering in the stomach, the tightness in the chest, the heat in the face. It requires building the capacity to tolerate discomfort without immediately reaching for a distraction or a numbing agent.

## The Architecture of the Eruption

Let us look closer at the physical architecture of your anger. When the threat is perceived - whether it is a boundary violation, an injustice, or a deep unmet need - the amygdala sounds the alarm. The hypothalamus signals the adrenal glands. Cortisol and adrenaline flood the bloodstream. 

Your sympathetic nervous system kicks into high gear. Blood is shunted away from your digestive organs and toward your extremities. Your muscles tense, preparing to fight or flee. Your breath becomes shallow and rapid, drawing in more oxygen for the anticipated exertion. 

This is a brilliant, elegant system. It is designed to keep you alive. But in our modern world, the threats are rarely physical predators. They are emotional, relational, systemic. And because we cannot physically fight or flee from a toxic work environment, a dismissive partner, or a culture of oppression, the physiological charge of the anger gets trapped in the body.

The psoas muscle, deep in the core of your body, connecting your lumbar spine to your femur, is intimately involved in the fight-or-flight response. It is the muscle that allows you to curl into a fetal position to protect your vital organs, or to sprint away from danger. When anger is suppressed, the psoas remains chronically contracted. This can lead to lower back pain, hip tension, and a feeling of deep, unshakeable unease. 

To release the psoas, we must first acknowledge the anger. We must tell the body that we hear its message. We must validate the physiological response. "Yes, I feel the heat. Yes, I feel the tension. It makes sense that I am angry."

## The Myth of the "Good" Woman

The myth of the "good" woman is one of the most effective tools of suppression ever invented. The good woman is accommodating. She is quiet. She is always smiling. She puts the needs of others before her own. She does not get angry. If she does get angry, she expresses it in a way that is palatable, reasonable, and non-threatening.

This myth is a straightjacket for the soul. It forces us to amputate a vital part of our humanity. It teaches us to fear our own power. 

Pele is not a "good" woman. She is a force of nature. She is passionate, volatile, and fiercely protective of her domain. She does not apologize for her eruptions. She knows that her fire is necessary for the ongoing creation of the world.

When we reclaim our anger, we are reclaiming our inner Pele. We are rejecting the straightjacket of the "good" woman. We are embracing the full spectrum of our emotional and physiological reality. We are saying, "I am not here to be comfortable for you. I am here to be true to myself."

This is not an easy path. It requires courage. It requires a willingness to be misunderstood, to be labeled as "difficult" or "too much." But the alternative is a slow, silent death of the spirit. The alternative is a body that is rigid with suppressed emotion, a jaw that is permanently clenched, a heart that is closed off from the world.

## The Fire That Cleanses

In many indigenous traditions, fire is seen as a cleansing agent. It burns away the impurities, leaving behind only what is essential. Your anger can serve the same purpose. 

When you allow your anger to burn clean - without the toxic fuel of blame, resentment, or self-pity - it can clarify your boundaries. It can illuminate your deepest values. It can give you the energy to make necessary changes in your life.

Think of a time when your anger propelled you to take a stand, to say "no," to walk away from a situation that was harming you. That was the sacred fire of Pele working through you. That was your body's wisdom guiding you toward safety and integrity.

We must learn to tend this fire with respect. We must not let it rage out of control, consuming everything in its path. But we must also not extinguish it. We must build a hearth for it in our bodies, a safe container where it can burn bright and warm.

This hearth is built through somatic awareness. It is built through practices like the one described above, where we ground ourselves in the earth and allow the energy to move through us. It is built through the support of nervine herbs that strengthen our capacity to hold the heat. It is built through the understanding that our anger is not a flaw, but a feature of our brilliant, resilient biology.

## The Aftermath of the Eruption

After a volcano erupts, there is a period of stillness. The earth is scorched, but it is also deeply nourished. The ash that falls from the sky is rich in minerals, creating the perfect conditions for new life to emerge.

The same is true for our own eruptions. After we have allowed our anger to move through us, there is often a feeling of exhaustion, but also a feeling of profound relief. The tension in the jaw releases. The breath deepens. The body feels lighter, more spacious.

This is the time for rest. This is the time for integration. This is the time to nourish the body with gentle foods, with soothing herbs, with quiet time in nature. 

Do not rush this process. Do not immediately try to figure out what to do next. Just allow yourself to be in the aftermath. Allow the new soil to settle. 

In time, new growth will emerge. New insights, new boundaries, new ways of relating to yourself and the world. This is the gift of Pele. This is the sacred alchemy of anger.

## The Anatomy of the Jaw

Let us return to the jaw. The masseter muscle. It is here that so much of our unexpressed anger is stored. When we bite our tongues, when we swallow our words, the masseter bears the brunt of the effort. 

Over time, this chronic tension can lead to TMJ, headaches, and a feeling of being constantly "on edge." The jaw becomes a physical manifestation of our suppressed fury.

To release the jaw, we must first become aware of it. Throughout the day, check in with your masseter. Is it tight? Are your teeth clenched? 

If so, do not force it to relax. Simply bring your awareness to the area. Imagine a warm, soothing light bathing the muscle. Allow your lower jaw to drop slightly, creating a small space between your upper and lower teeth. 

You can also gently massage the masseter with your fingertips. Apply firm, steady pressure to the areas that feel tight or tender. As you massage, take deep, slow breaths into your belly. 

This simple practice can have a profound effect on your nervous system. By releasing the tension in the jaw, you are sending a signal to your brain that it is safe to relax. You are telling your body that the threat has passed.

## The Role of the Diaphragm

The diaphragm is another key player in the architecture of anger. This large, dome-shaped muscle sits at the base of the ribcage, separating the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. It is the primary muscle of respiration.

When we are angry, our breathing often becomes shallow and rapid. We breathe into our upper chest, rather than deep into our bellies. This shallow breathing keeps the body in a state of sympathetic arousal, perpetuating the fight-or-flight response.

To shift out of this state, we must engage the diaphragm. We must learn to breathe deeply, expanding the belly on the inhale and contracting it on the exhale. 

This type of diaphragmatic breathing stimulates the vagus nerve, which runs from the brainstem down into the abdomen. The vagus nerve is the primary component of the parasympathetic nervous system, the "rest and digest" system. 

When we stimulate the vagus nerve through deep breathing, we send a powerful signal of safety to the body. We calm the amygdala, lower our heart rate, and reduce the levels of cortisol and adrenaline in our bloodstream.

This is not about suppressing the anger. It is about creating a physiological container that is strong enough to hold the anger without being overwhelmed by it. It is about building the capacity to stay present with the heat, rather than being consumed by it.

## The Wisdom of the Earth

Ultimately, the journey of reclaiming our anger is a journey of returning to the earth. It is a recognition that we are not separate from nature, but an integral part of it. 

Our bodies are made of the same elements as the earth. Our bones are made of minerals. Our blood is made of water. Our breath is made of air. And our anger is made of fire.

When we honor our anger, we are honoring the earth within us. We are acknowledging the wild, untamed forces that pulse through our veins. We are reclaiming our birthright as creatures of this planet.

Pele is not just a myth. She is a living reality. She is the magma that flows beneath the surface of the earth, and she is the magma that flows beneath the surface of our skin. 

May we learn to listen to her wisdom. May we learn to tend her fire with respect. May we learn to trust the sacred anger of volcanoes.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I let my anger out, I will destroy everything in my life. How do I stop being afraid of it?**
**A:** This is the most common fear, and it makes perfect physiological sense. Your body has equated anger with danger for a very long time. The key is not to "let it all out" at once. That is an explosion. The key is titration - feeling a tiny drop of the anger, noticing the heat in your jaw or your chest, and then grounding yourself. You build the capacity to hold the sensation without acting on it immediately. You are not destroying your life; you are simply feeling your own biology.

**Q: You mentioned herbs for the liver. Can I just take a supplement?**
**A:** You can, but you miss the relationship. Herbs are not pharmaceuticals; they are living intelligences. When you brew a strong infusion of dandelion root, you smell the earth. You taste the profound bitterness. That bitterness hits the receptors on your tongue and sends an immediate signal to your vagus nerve and your liver to start moving stagnation. A pill bypasses the sensory experience, and the sensory experience is half the medicine.

**Q: What if my anger is directed at someone who is no longer in my life, or someone who has died?**
**A:** The body does not know they are gone. The nervous system operates in the eternal present tense. The charge is still trapped in your psoas, your diaphragm, your fascia. You do not need the other person to process the anger. The anger belongs to you, not them. It is your life force. By doing the somatic practices, by making the guttural sounds, by moving the energy through your tissues, you are completing the stress cycle that was interrupted years ago.

**Q: Is it possible to be too angry?**
**A:** It is possible to be consumed by the secondary emotions that attach to anger - resentment, bitterness, the story of victimhood. Those will rot your tissues. But the pure, raw, somatic sensation of anger? No. It is just energy. It is just heat. When you strip away the story and just feel the physical sensation, it usually moves through the body in less than two minutes. It is our resistance to it, and the stories we spin about it, that make it last for decades.

**Q: How do I know the difference between intuition and just being triggered?**
**A:** A trigger feels frantic, urgent, and usually pulls you up into your chest and throat. It feels like a buzzing, chaotic energy. True somatic intuition - even when it is angry - feels grounded. It drops into the belly. It feels heavy, solid, and undeniable. A trigger says, "I have to fix this right now or I will die." Intuition says, "This is unacceptable, and I will handle it with the full weight of my presence."

## The Deeper Layers of Fascia

When we talk about the body keeping the score, we must talk about fascia. Fascia is the connective tissue that wraps around every muscle, every organ, every nerve in your body. It is a continuous web of tissue that holds you together. And it is highly innervated. It is rich in sensory receptors. 

When you experience a trauma, or when you chronically suppress an emotion like anger, the fascia responds. It tightens. It thickens. It loses its elasticity. It becomes rigid, creating a physical armor against the perceived threat. 

This fascial armoring can restrict your movement, alter your posture, and create chronic pain. It can also trap the emotional charge in the tissues. 

To release the fascia, we cannot use force. If you try to force tight fascia to stretch, it will simply contract further in self-defense. We must use patience. We must use sustained, gentle pressure. We must use the breath. 

When you breathe deeply into the belly, you are gently stretching the fascial web from the inside out. You are sending a signal of safety to the tissues, allowing them to soften and release their grip. 

This is slow work. It is not a quick fix. It requires a willingness to be present with the subtle sensations of the body, to listen to the whispers of the tissues before they become screams. 

But as the fascia releases, as the armor softens, you may find that the trapped emotions also begin to move. You may experience a sudden wave of heat, a tremor in the muscles, a spontaneous release of tears or laughter. 

This is the body healing itself. This is the magma finding a safe channel to the surface. 

## The Alchemy of Presence

The ultimate goal of this work is not to become a person who never gets angry. That is the goal of the "good" woman myth. The goal is to become a person who can be fully present with their anger, without being consumed by it and without suppressing it. 

It is to become a person who can feel the heat rising in the belly, the tension gripping the jaw, the heart rate accelerating, and say, "Ah, there is the fire. I know this fire. It is mine."

It is to become a person who can use the energy of the anger to set clear boundaries, to speak their truth, to protect what is sacred to them. 

This is the alchemy of presence. It is the ability to transform the raw, destructive power of the volcano into the creative, life-sustaining power of the hearth. 

It is a lifelong practice. There will be times when you forget. There will be times when you swallow the anger, when you tighten the throat, when you try to cap the volcano. 

When that happens, do not judge yourself. Do not add the burden of shame to the burden of suppressed emotion. Simply notice what you have done. Take a deep breath. Feel your feet on the earth. And begin again. 

The fire will always be there, waiting for you to claim it. Pele is patient. She knows that the earth will eventually move. She knows that the magma will eventually rise. 

And when it does, may you be ready to receive it. May you be ready to feel the sacred, terrifying, beautiful power of your own biology.

---
